nodes.name: Pen Names
nodes.description: Literary pseudonyms and the real names behind them

items.prompt: What was the real name of the American humorist who wrote *Adventures of Huckleberry Finn* under the pen name Mark Twain?
items.explanation:
American author (1835–1910); born in Florida, Missouri, grew up in Hannibal on the Mississippi River
Adopted 'Mark Twain' in 1863 — Mississippi River pilot slang for two fathoms (12 feet) of safe water depth
Also wrote *The Adventures of Tom Sawyer* (1876) and *A Connecticut Yankee in King Arthur's Court* (1889)
First American author to write a novel on a typewriter (*Tom Sawyer*, submitted to publisher 1875)
Born the year Halley's Comet appeared; predicted he would die when it returned — and did (April 21, 1910)
Friendship with Nikola Tesla; financial ruin from bad investments, including a failed typesetting machine

items.prompt: What was the real name of the Victorian novelist who wrote *Middlemarch* and *The Mill on the Floss* under the pen name George Eliot?
items.explanation:
English novelist (1819–1880); born in Warwickshire, later known socially as 'Marian'
Chose a male pen name to ensure her work was taken seriously and not dismissed as 'light' women's fiction
*Middlemarch* (1871–72) is frequently ranked the greatest novel in the English language by critics
Also wrote *Silas Marner* (1861), *Daniel Deronda* (1876), and the poem *O May I Join the Choir Invisible*
Lived for 24 years with philosopher George Henry Lewes (who was married but separated) — the relationship was socially scandalous
Married her much younger friend John Cross just seven months before her death at 61

items.prompt: What was the real name of the British author who wrote *1984* and *Animal Farm* under the pen name George Orwell?
items.explanation:
British author (1903–1950); born in Motihari, Bengal, India, to a colonial civil servant
Adopted 'George Orwell' in 1933 for *Down and Out in Paris and London* — chosen partly to spare his family embarrassment
*Animal Farm* (1945) was rejected by multiple publishers, including Victor Gollancz, for its anti-Soviet content during WWII
*1984* (1949) introduced 'Big Brother,' 'doublethink,' 'Newspeak,' and 'Room 101' into the English language
Fought in the Spanish Civil War (1937) with the POUM militia; shot through the throat by a sniper
Died of tuberculosis in January 1950, six months after *1984* was published, aged 46

items.prompt: What was the real name of the English author who wrote *Alice's Adventures in Wonderland* under the pen name Lewis Carroll?
items.explanation:
English author and mathematician (1832–1898); lecturer in mathematics at Christ Church, Oxford
Pen name derived by Latinizing his first two names: Charles Lutwidge → Carolus Ludovicus → Lewis Carroll
First told the Alice story in 1862 during a boat trip with the Liddell family; Alice Liddell inspired the protagonist
*Through the Looking-Glass* (1871) introduced Tweedledee and Tweedledum, Humpty Dumpty, and the poem 'Jabberwocky'
Invented the word 'chortle' (a portmanteau of 'chuckle' and 'snort') in 'Jabberwocky'
Also a pioneering portrait photographer and the inventor of the word ladder puzzle

items.prompt: What was the real name of the French Enlightenment writer who published *Candide* under the pen name Voltaire?
items.explanation:
French Enlightenment writer and philosopher (1694–1778); born in Paris
Adopted 'Voltaire' around 1718 — likely an anagram of 'AROVET LI' (Latinized name with 'le jeune')
*Candide* (1759) satirizes Leibnizian optimism ('best of all possible worlds') in response to the Lisbon earthquake (1755)
Imprisoned twice in the Bastille; exiled to England (1726–1729), where he absorbed Locke and Newton
Prolific correspondent — over 20,000 letters survive; also wrote the *Dictionnaire philosophique* (1764)
Died in Paris at 83 after returning from 28 years of exile; the Catholic Church refused him a church burial

items.prompt: What was the real name of the French playwright who wrote *Tartuffe* and *The Misanthrope* under the pen name Molière?
items.explanation:
French playwright and actor (1622–1673); born in Paris to an upholsterer who served the royal court
Adopted 'Molière' around 1644 — possibly to spare his family's bourgeois reputation from his theatrical career
Patron was Louis XIV, who personally intervened to allow *Tartuffe* (1664) to be performed after church objections
*The Misanthrope* (1666) is considered his most philosophically serious work alongside the comic masterpiece
Died on stage — or shortly after — during the fourth performance of his play *Le Malade Imaginaire* (February 17, 1673)
Burned at least two early plays; most of the surviving canon was written in a 13-year burst (1659–1673)

items.prompt: What was the real name of the French Realist novelist who wrote *The Red and the Black* under the pen name Stendhal?
items.explanation:
French novelist (1783–1842); born in Grenoble; used over 100 pen names but 'Stendhal' became permanent
'Stendhal' taken from the Prussian town of Stendal, birthplace of art historian Johann Winckelmann
*The Red and the Black* (1830) follows Julien Sorel's social climbing in Restoration France; 'red' = military, 'black' = clergy
*The Charterhouse of Parma* (1839) was written in 52 days; Balzac praised it as a masterpiece
Served in Napoleon's army; witnessed the burning of Moscow (1812) during the retreat from Russia
'Stendhal syndrome' — the psychosomatic response to overwhelming art — is named after his account of dizziness in Florence (1817)

items.prompt: What was the real name of the French Romantic novelist who published under the pen name George Sand?
items.explanation:
French Romantic novelist (1804–1876); prolific author of over 70 novels including *Indiana* (1832) and *Consuelo* (1842)
Chose a male pen name to publish freely in a literary world that dismissed women writers
Also wore men's clothing in public — which was legally restricted in Paris — and smoked cigars openly
Nine-year relationship with composer Frédéric Chopin (1838–1847); nursed him through illness in Majorca
Friendships spanned Balzac, Flaubert, Turgenev, Delacroix, and Liszt — a central figure of 19th-century Paris
Died at her estate Nohant; the estate is now a museum and she is buried in its garden

items.prompt: What was the real name of the Danish author who wrote *Out of Africa* under the pen name Isak Dinesen?
items.explanation:
Danish author (1885–1962); born Karen Christence Dinesen near Rungsted, Denmark
Used 'Isak Dinesen' (her father's surname) for English publications; also published as 'Pierre Andrézel' in Danish
*Out of Africa* (1937) recounts 17 years managing a coffee farm in British East Africa (Kenya) after her marriage ended
*Babette's Feast* (1958) was adapted into an Academy Award–winning Danish film (1987) — only Danish film to win Best Foreign Language Film
Nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature multiple times; Ernest Hemingway reportedly said she deserved it over him (1954)
Suffered lifelong ill health from syphilis contracted from her husband Baron Bror Blixen early in their marriage

items.prompt: What was the real name of the American short story writer who published *The Gift of the Magi* under the pen name O. Henry?
items.explanation:
American short story writer (1862–1910); born in Greensboro, North Carolina
Adopted 'O. Henry' while serving a prison sentence (1898–1901) for embezzlement from a Texas bank — the circumstances remain disputed
Wrote most of his ~600 stories in the last decade of his life, many for the New York World Sunday supplement
*The Gift of the Magi* (1905) is the archetypal O. Henry story — famous for its ironic twist ending
The O. Henry Award for short fiction, given annually since 1919, is named in his honor
Died of cirrhosis at 47; his last words reportedly included 'Turn up the lights — I don't want to go home in the dark'

items.prompt: What was the real name of the British Edwardian short story writer known for dark wit who published under the pen name Saki?
items.explanation:
British author (1870–1916); born in Akyab, Burma, to a British army officer; raised by strict aunts in Devon
Pen name 'Saki' likely taken from the cupbearer in the *Rubáiyát of Omar Khayyám*
Known for macabre, witty short stories such as 'The Open Window,' 'Sredni Vashtar,' and 'The Interlopers'
Many stories feature revenge of children or animals against cruel or pompous adults — drawn from his joyless childhood
Enlisted as a private in WWI at age 43 (above draft age) despite being eligible for officer rank
Killed by a German sniper's bullet on November 14, 1916, at the Battle of the Ancre; his last words were reportedly 'Put that bloody cigarette out'

items.prompt: What was the real name of the Russian-American novelist who wrote *Atlas Shrugged* and *The Fountainhead* under the pen name Ayn Rand?
items.explanation:
Russian-American novelist and philosopher (1905–1982); born in Saint Petersburg
Adopted 'Ayn Rand' while emigrating — 'Ayn' possibly from a Finnish author's name; 'Rand' from her Remington Rand typewriter
*The Fountainhead* (1943) was rejected by 12 publishers before becoming a bestseller; *Atlas Shrugged* (1957) is her magnum opus
Developed Objectivism, a philosophy advocating rational self-interest, laissez-faire capitalism, and rejection of altruism
Fled Soviet Russia in 1926; her family's pharmacy had been seized by the Bolsheviks after the revolution
Died in New York in 1982; *Atlas Shrugged* consistently ranks among the most influential books in the United States

items.prompt: What was the real name of the Polish-British novelist who wrote *Heart of Darkness* and *Lord Jim* under the pen name Joseph Conrad?
items.explanation:
Polish-British novelist (1857–1924); born in Berdychiv, Ukraine (then Russian-controlled Poland)
Anglicized his third given name 'Konrad' to 'Conrad' for publication; English was his third language after Polish and French
*Heart of Darkness* (1899) draws on his 1890 journey up the Congo River as a steamboat captain for a Belgian trading company
Became a British subject in 1886 after a career as a merchant mariner; didn't publish his first novel until age 38
*Lord Jim* (1900), *Nostromo* (1904), and *The Secret Agent* (1907) cement his place in the English literary canon
Francis Ford Coppola's *Apocalypse Now* (1979) is an adaptation of *Heart of Darkness* set during the Vietnam War

items.prompt: What was the real name of the Chilean poet who won the 1971 Nobel Prize in Literature under the pen name Pablo Neruda?
items.explanation:
Chilean poet and diplomat (1904–1973); born in Parral, Chile
Adopted 'Pablo Neruda' as a teenager — taken from Czech poet Jan Neruda — to hide his writing from his disapproving father
Best known for *Veinte poemas de amor y una canción desesperada* (1924) and *Canto General* (1950)
Awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1971; Gabriel García Márquez called him 'the greatest poet of the 20th century in any language'
Served as a Communist senator and Chilean consul in various countries; forced into exile after a 1948 government crackdown
Died September 23, 1973, twelve days after Pinochet's coup; circumstances of death (officially prostate cancer) remain disputed

items.prompt: What was the real name of the author who published *Jane Eyre* (1847) under the male pen name Currer Bell?
items.explanation:
English novelist (1816–1855); eldest of the three writing Brontë sisters; born in Thornton, Yorkshire
All three sisters — Charlotte, Emily, and Anne — initially published under male pen names: Currer, Ellis, and Acton Bell
Chosen to avoid the 'condescension' shown to female authors; 'Bell' preserved their real initials (C.B., E.B., A.B.)
*Jane Eyre* (1847) was an immediate bestseller; the mystery of 'Currer Bell's' identity was a London literary sensation
Also wrote *Shirley* (1849), *Villette* (1853), and *The Professor* (posthumous, 1857)
Died in 1855, aged 38, during early pregnancy; three of her four siblings died between 1848 and 1849
